past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well controlled on medication
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago

medications.frequency: As needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Father died at age 82 from complications of Parkinson's disease, diagnosed at age 73
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died at age 79 from myocardial infarction
One sister, age 62, with essential tremor
No family history of dementia, stroke, or other neurodegenerative disease aside from father's Parkinson's

social_history.occupation: Retired high school history teacher, retired 3 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Glass of red wine with dinner 2-3 times per week
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home; wife is his primary companion and has accompanied him to this visit
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 2-3 miles daily but has reduced this over the past year due to unsteadiness
social_history.hobbies: Enjoys woodworking but reports increasing difficulty with fine hand movements over the past year

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is hypophonic with mild monotone quality but fluent and without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Mini-Mental State Examination score 28/30 (lost one point on serial sevens and one point on figure copy). Affect is mildly flat but appropriate. No evidence of depression on screening questioning, though wife notes he has become more withdrawn over the past year.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild hypomimia noted with reduced spontaneous blinking. Facial movements symmetric but diminished in amplitude. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Increased tone in the right upper extremity with cogwheel rigidity at the wrist; mild rigidity at the right elbow with activation maneuver (contralateral hand opening and closing). Left upper extremity tone mildly increased at the wrist. Lower extremity tone mildly increased bilaterally, right greater than left. Strength 5/5 in all extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements (finger tapping, hand pronation-supination, toe tapping) reveal decrement in speed and amplitude on the right side, with progressive reduction over 10 seconds. Left side shows mild slowing but better amplitude maintenance. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait notable for reduced right arm swing and mildly shortened stride length. Slight forward-flexed posture. Mild shuffling on initiation. Turns require 4-5 steps (en bloc turning). No festination. Pull test shows one step retropulsion but self-corrects. Tandem gait mildly impaired. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Resting tremor observed in the right hand at 4-5 Hz, most prominent when the patient is seated with hands resting in the lap and during ambulation. Tremor has a pill-rolling quality. Tremor suppressed with intentional movement and reappears after a brief latency on posture holding (re-emergent tremor). Mild intermittent resting tremor noted in the right lower extremity. No action tremor. Handwriting sample shows progressive micrographia. Glabellar tap sign positive (Myerson sign) with failure to habituate after 10 taps.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive right hand tremor and difficulty with fine motor tasks for the past 2 years, with worsening gait unsteadiness over the past year
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Thompson is a 65-year-old right-handed retired high school history teacher who presents to the outpatient neurology clinic with a two-year history of progressive right hand tremor and increasing difficulty with fine motor tasks. His wife first noticed a subtle intermittent tremor in his right hand approximately two years ago while he was watching television. The tremor was initially sporadic and barely perceptible, occurring primarily at rest, and the patient himself was largely unaware of it. Over the ensuing months, the tremor became more persistent and noticeable, particularly when he was sitting quietly or walking. He reports that the tremor diminishes when he actively uses his hand but returns shortly after he stops the voluntary movement.

Over the past 12 to 18 months, Mr. Thompson has noticed progressive difficulty with activities requiring manual dexterity. He reports that buttoning his shirts has become notably slower, particularly with the right hand, and his wife has begun assisting him with the top buttons. His handwriting, once neat and precise from decades of writing on chalkboards, has become progressively smaller and more cramped, to the point where he finds it difficult to write legibly on greeting cards. He has also noticed that his woodworking hobby, which he took up enthusiastically after retirement, has become increasingly frustrating as he has difficulty manipulating small tools and hardware with his right hand.

In the past year, both the patient and his wife have noted changes in his walking. She describes that he seems to shuffle his feet more, particularly when starting to walk, and that his right arm does not swing naturally at his side as it once did. He has had two near-falls in the past three months, once when turning quickly in the kitchen and once when stepping off a curb, though he did not actually fall to the ground on either occasion. He denies any frank loss of consciousness, lightheadedness, or vertigo preceding these episodes. His wife also reports that his voice has become quieter over the past year, and she frequently asks him to repeat himself. She notes that his facial expressions seem less animated than before, though she initially attributed this to his being quieter in retirement.

The patient denies any cognitive complaints, though his wife mentions that he occasionally seems slower to respond in conversation than he used to. He denies hallucinations, vivid dreams, or acting out dreams in his sleep. He denies difficulty swallowing, drooling, constipation, or urinary urgency beyond his known BPH symptoms. He denies any exposure to antipsychotic medications, antiemetics, or other dopamine-blocking agents. There is no history of encephalitis, significant head trauma, or exposure to toxins such as manganese or MPTP.

findings.location: Bilateral cerebral hemispheres, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal gray-white matter differentiation throughout. No focal signal abnormalities in the basal ganglia or substantia nigra.
signal_characteristics.T2: No focal or confluent signal abnormalities. Normal signal in the putamen and caudate bilaterally.
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No periventricular or deep white matter hyperintensities beyond minimal age-appropriate changes.
Scattered punctate white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular and subcortical regions, consistent with mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes appropriate for age.

additional_observations: No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy; Evans index 0.28 (normal <0.30)
No basal ganglia infarcts or calcifications
No mass lesion, hemorrhage, or acute infarction
No hydrocephalus features to suggest NPH
Normal midbrain morphology without hummingbird sign or hot cross bun sign
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI for age. Minimal chronic small vessel ischemic changes typical for a 65-year-old patient with hypertension. No structural cause for parkinsonism identified. No evidence of vascular parkinsonism, normal pressure hydrocephalus, or atypical parkinsonian syndromes such as MSA or PSP. The absence of structural pathology is consistent with idiopathic Parkinson's disease.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - normal MRI is expected in PD; imaging primarily used to exclude secondary causes
Low - no strategic basal ganglia infarcts
Low - no ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy
Low - no pontine or cerebellar atrophy, no hot cross bun sign, no putaminal rim sign
Low - no midbrain atrophy, no hummingbird sign

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with neurological examination findings
Consider DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT) if clinical diagnosis is uncertain
Follow-up imaging only if clinical course is atypical

Thyroid_and_Additional.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function; hypothyroidism excluded as cause of psychomotor slowing
Normal B12; deficiency excluded as cause of neurological symptoms
Syphilis excluded as cause of parkinsonism
Normal ceruloplasmin; Wilson disease not suspected at this age but excluded

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel within normal limits. Thyroid function tests normal, excluding hypothyroidism as a cause of psychomotor slowing. Vitamin B12 level normal. RPR non-reactive. Ceruloplasmin within normal range. No metabolic or infectious etiology identified for the patient's parkinsonian symptoms.

findings.location: Bilateral striatum, asymmetric with greater reduction on the left (contralateral to clinically more affected right side)

signal_characteristics.left_putamen: Markedly reduced tracer uptake with near-complete loss of putaminal signal, resulting in a period-shaped (dot-like) pattern rather than the normal comma-shaped pattern
signal_characteristics.right_putamen: Moderately reduced tracer uptake in the posterior putamen with relative preservation of the anterior putamen

additional_observations: Asymmetric pattern of dopamine transporter loss is characteristic of Parkinson's disease
Posterior-to-anterior gradient of putaminal involvement is typical of neurodegenerative parkinsonism
Left-greater-than-right involvement is concordant with the patient's right-predominant motor symptoms
output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating bilateral asymmetric reduction in striatal dopamine transporter uptake, left greater than right, with a posterior-to-anterior gradient. This pattern is consistent with a presynaptic dopaminergic deficit as seen in Parkinson's disease and supports the clinical diagnosis. The findings effectively exclude essential tremor and drug-induced parkinsonism, in which DaTscan would be normal.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - asymmetric putaminal loss with posterior-to-anterior gradient is classic
Low but cannot be excluded by DaTscan alone; clinical features are more consistent with PD
Excluded - DaTscan would be normal in ET

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with motor examination findings
Consider levodopa challenge test to confirm dopaminergic responsiveness
Follow-up clinical assessment in 3-6 months to monitor disease progression and treatment response

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Moderate motor impairment at baseline, predominantly right-sided
50% improvement in motor score after acute levodopa challenge, indicating robust dopaminergic responsiveness
Robust positive response strongly supports diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and predicts good long-term levodopa responsiveness

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.value: Mild nausea (self-limited, no treatment required), no dyskinesia
Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Mild GI side effects are common with initial levodopa exposure; absence of dyskinesia at this stage is expected

output.interpretation: Acute levodopa challenge test demonstrates a robust 50% improvement in UPDRS Part III motor score (from 28 to 14), well exceeding the 30% threshold for a positive response. Tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia all showed meaningful improvement. This robust dopaminergic responsiveness strongly supports the diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and distinguishes it from atypical parkinsonian syndromes such as MSA and PSP, which typically show poor or absent levodopa response.
abnormal_values_summary: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III 14 (50% improvement, strongly positive response)

Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.test: Tilt table test - standing BP (3 minutes)
Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.clinical_significance: Systolic drop of 12 mmHg is within normal limits; no significant orthostatic hypotension

Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.test: Heart rate variability with deep breathing

Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.value: Normal sweat volumes at all four sites

Autonomic_Reflex_Testing.clinical_significance: Normal cardiovagal and adrenergic function during Valsalva maneuver

output.interpretation: Autonomic reflex testing demonstrates intact cardiovagal, adrenergic, and sudomotor function. No evidence of significant orthostatic hypotension or autonomic failure. These findings are reassuring and argue against multiple system atrophy (MSA), in which early and prominent autonomic failure would be expected. Mild autonomic dysfunction may develop later in the course of Parkinson's disease, but the current normal results are consistent with early-stage idiopathic PD.

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Low-normal score; lost points on trail-making (executive) and clock drawing tasks
Average overall intellectual functioning, consistent with educational background
Low average processing speed, likely reflecting bradyphrenia associated with early Parkinson's disease
Low average executive functioning; mildly slowed set-shifting

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological evaluation reveals overall average cognitive functioning with mildly reduced processing speed and low-average executive function, a profile commonly seen in early Parkinson's disease (PD-related mild cognitive changes). Memory, language, and visuospatial functions are preserved. This cognitive profile does not meet criteria for PD-MCI at present but warrants longitudinal monitoring. The pattern of frontal-subcortical slowing without cortical memory deficits is characteristic of PD and argues against Alzheimer's disease or Lewy body dementia with prominent cortical involvement.
abnormal_values_summary: Processing Speed Index 88 (low average) - consistent with bradyphrenia in early PD

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Reduced sleep efficiency with increased wakefulness after sleep onset

Polysomnography.value: Present - sustained tonic chin EMG activity during 45% of REM epochs, with phasic EMG bursts in upper and lower extremities
Polysomnography.reference_range: Tonic: <30% of REM epochs; Phasic: <15% of REM mini-epochs
Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Electrographic evidence of REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD). RSWA with associated dream enactment behavior (arm reaching and vocalization captured on video) during one REM period.
Mildly elevated PLMI, commonly associated with Parkinson's disease and may contribute to sleep fragmentation

output.interpretation: Polysomnography demonstrates electrographic and clinical evidence of REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD) with REM sleep without atonia and captured dream enactment behavior. RBD is strongly associated with synucleinopathies including Parkinson's disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, and multiple system atrophy, and may precede motor symptoms by years. In this clinical context, the presence of RBD further supports the diagnosis of Parkinson's disease as the underlying synucleinopathy. Mild periodic limb movements in sleep are also noted. No significant obstructive sleep apnea.
abnormal_values_summary: REM sleep without atonia present in 45% of REM epochs with dream enactment behavior - diagnostic of REM sleep behavior disorder

output.query: Parkinson's disease initial treatment levodopa dopamine agonist

results.title: Levodopa and the Progression of Parkinson's Disease (LEAP Trial)
results.source: Verschuur et al., N Engl J Med 2019;380(4):315-324
results.summary: Early versus delayed levodopa initiation did not affect the rate of disease progression in early PD. Levodopa provided sustained symptomatic benefit without accelerating neurodegeneration.

results.title: PD MED Trial - Long-term results of initiating levodopa, dopamine agonists, or MAO-B inhibitors
results.summary: Levodopa-based therapy was associated with better patient-rated quality of life compared to initial treatment with dopamine agonists or MAO-B inhibitors in early PD.

results.title: Movement Disorder Society Evidence-Based Review of Treatments for Parkinson's Disease
results.summary: Levodopa is the most effective symptomatic treatment for PD. MAO-B inhibitors and dopamine agonists are alternatives for mild symptoms. Amantadine is efficacious for dyskinesia management in later stages.

output.summary: Current evidence supports levodopa as the most effective initial treatment for Parkinson's disease, particularly when functional impairment is present. The LEAP trial dispelled the historical concern that early levodopa accelerates disease progression. For patients under 65-70 with mild symptoms, MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline) or dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine) may be considered as initial therapy to delay levodopa-associated motor complications, though quality of life data favor early levodopa. Combination therapy and dose optimization should be guided by symptom severity and functional impact.

contraindications: Non-selective MAO inhibitors (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) - risk of hypertensive crisis; must be discontinued at least 2 weeks before starting levodopa
warnings: Iron supplements and high-protein meals may reduce levodopa absorption; advise taking 30-60 minutes before meals
May cause orthostatic hypotension, particularly in combination with tamsulosin (both can lower blood pressure); monitor BP after initiation
Nausea is common at initiation; taking with a small carbohydrate snack may help, though high-protein foods should be avoided
Monitor for impulse control disorders (more common with dopamine agonists but can occur with levodopa)
May cause vivid dreams or hallucinations, particularly at higher doses or in elderly patients
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Rasagiline 1 mg daily (MAO-B inhibitor, for mild symptoms)
Pramipexole 0.125 mg three times daily, titrate gradually (dopamine agonist)
Ropinirole 0.25 mg three times daily, titrate gradually (dopamine agonist)
Rotigotine transdermal patch 2 mg/24 hours (dopamine agonist, once daily patch)

differential.key_features: Essential tremor typically presents as a bilateral action/postural tremor without bradykinesia or rigidity. This patient has a unilateral resting tremor with clear cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia with decrement, shuffling gait, and micrographia, which are not features of essential tremor.

No exposure to dopamine-blocking agents (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine). Drug-induced parkinsonism is typically symmetric and reversible upon drug withdrawal. This patient's symptoms are asymmetric and progressive.

MSA typically presents with early prominent autonomic failure (severe orthostatic hypotension, urinary retention, erectile dysfunction), cerebellar signs, and poor levodopa response. This patient has no significant autonomic failure, no cerebellar findings, and the clinical presentation is classic for idiopathic PD.

PSP is characterized by early postural instability and falls, vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, axial rigidity greater than limb rigidity, and poor levodopa response. This patient has predominantly limb-onset asymmetric parkinsonism without gaze palsy or early falls.

NPH classically presents with the triad of gait apraxia (magnetic gait), urinary incontinence, and dementia. MRI would show ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy. This patient's MRI shows no ventriculomegaly, and his gait pattern is parkinsonian rather than apraxic.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of motor and non-motor symptoms including tremor characteristics, bradykinesia, gait changes, handwriting changes, and any autonomic, sleep, or cognitive complaints
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive asymmetric resting tremor with bradykinesia, micrographia, shuffling gait, and reduced arm swing - classic parkinsonian motor syndrome

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with focus on extrapyramidal signs including tone assessment with activation maneuver, rapid alternating movements with assessment of decrement, gait analysis, and postural stability testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric cogwheel rigidity (right > left), bradykinesia with decrement on repetitive movements, resting pill-rolling tremor, shuffling gait with reduced right arm swing, hypomimia, and hypophonia

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI for age, excluding vascular parkinsonism, NPH, and imaging features of atypical parkinsonian syndromes

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, RPR, and ceruloplasmin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal laboratory values, excluding metabolic and reversible causes of parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Consider DaTscan if clinical diagnosis is uncertain or to distinguish from essential tremor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced dopamine transporter uptake in the striatum, confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dopaminergic therapy with carbidopa-levodopa given functionally significant symptoms, after checking drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions with current medications; appropriate first-line therapy for PD with functional impairment

optimal_actions.action: Refer to physical therapy for gait training, balance exercises, and fall prevention
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Physical therapy referral placed for PD-specific exercise program

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient and family about disease nature, prognosis, treatment expectations, and the importance of regular exercise
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient and wife educated about PD, treatment plan, and support resources

optimal_actions.action: Screen for non-motor symptoms including depression, anxiety, REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, and cognitive changes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Screening identifies non-motor symptoms that may benefit from targeted treatment

optimal_actions.action: Schedule follow-up in 4-6 weeks to assess treatment response and titrate medication
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Follow-up plan established for medication optimization

critical_actions: Perform a detailed motor examination assessing for the cardinal features of parkinsonism: tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability
Assess asymmetry of motor findings, which is a key feature supporting idiopathic PD over other causes of parkinsonism
Order brain MRI to exclude structural and vascular causes of parkinsonism
Obtain basic labs to rule out metabolic causes such as hypothyroidism and Wilson disease
Review medication list to exclude drug-induced parkinsonism
Initiate appropriate dopaminergic therapy given functionally significant symptoms
Refer to physical therapy for gait and balance training
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing dopamine-blocking antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) which would worsen parkinsonism; use domperidone or ondansetron instead if antiemetics are needed

Prescribing typical or atypical antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine) which block dopamine receptors; if antipsychotics are needed, only quetiapine, pimavanserin, or clozapine are appropriate in PD
Abruptly discontinuing dopaminergic medications once started, which can precipitate neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome or parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome
Performing lumbar puncture as part of the routine workup for this clinical presentation (no indication)
Ordering a brain biopsy (diagnosis is clinical, supported by imaging and treatment response)
Withholding treatment when the patient has functionally significant symptoms affecting daily activities
key_reasoning_points: The asymmetric resting tremor with pill-rolling quality, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia with progressive decrement on repetitive movements constitute the classic motor triad of Parkinson's disease
Asymmetry of motor findings is one of the strongest clinical features supporting idiopathic PD over atypical parkinsonian syndromes, which tend to present more symmetrically
Micrographia is a highly specific sign of PD-related bradykinesia and is a useful clinical marker that patients can track at home
The resting nature of the tremor, its suppression with intentional movement, and re-emergence on sustained posture distinguish it from essential tremor, which is primarily an action/postural tremor
A positive family history (father with PD) increases the clinical likelihood, though most PD is sporadic; first-degree relatives have approximately 2-3 times the population risk
Normal brain MRI is expected in idiopathic PD and serves primarily to exclude alternative diagnoses such as vascular parkinsonism, NPH, and structural lesions
The absence of red flags such as early falls, rapid progression, early severe autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy, and poor levodopa response argues against atypical parkinsonian syndromes
Carbidopa-levodopa remains the most effective symptomatic treatment for PD, and current evidence (LEAP trial) demonstrates no disease-modifying harm from early initiation

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generator
metadata.estimated_complexity: Straightforward - classic presentation with asymmetric resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia
